text: For whatever reason, on April twenty first, twenty twenty, I put a phone on the roof of my car and I drove up PCH. It was something that, like much of the pandemic, I had tried to forget about until a couple weeks ago. As we know, January seventh changed the landscape of Southern California. Tragic loss of life and hundreds of years of California history destroyed in a matter of hours. And now, a chance to look at what was and hope for what could be. PCH is one of the all-time great drives. Heading up the coast, a right of passage for Angelenos. The surf, the sun, iconic. During the pandemic, I filmed a stretch of PCH from the hood of my car. There wasn't a lot to do, you might remember, but now, nearly five years later, everything is different. Topanga is where I started recording. On the left, the beach; to the right, the real inn. There was always a line, but while you waited, you could look at pictures on the wall of fires the restaurant had survived over the years. The Topanga Ranch Motel, built in nineteen twenty nine, Rosenthal Winery, Wiley's bait shop, and Chilata Thai. Just past Tuna Canyon Road is where Los Angeles ends and Malibu begins. Homes stood all along here, starting at nineteen thousand PCH. Now, home after home gone. Looking north, a view of the shoreline from the road. Behind the barriers, still law enforcement, national guardsmen, disaster cleanup companies, and utility companies like SoCal Edison, who were working on burned power poles. Past Big Rock Drive, rows and rows of Long Beach houses used to be here. Now, charred bricks, debris, and cars.
